content.text: What Decoder-Only Means
A decoder-only transformer generates text one token at a time by predicting what comes next. Each token can only attend to the tokens that came before it — never to future tokens. This
causal masking
is what makes auto-regressive generation possible: the model's prediction for position
t
is conditioned only on tokens 1 through
, so the same forward pass can compute all positions in parallel during training while remaining auto-regressive at inference.

attrs.alt: Left: lower-triangular causal attention mask showing each token attends only to past positions. Center: auto-regressive generation appending one token at a time until [EOS]. Right: GPT architecture (masked attention + FFN blocks, LM head) and model size comparison table.

content.text: Architecture
A GPT-style model has three parts:
Embedding layer:
token embedding + learned positional embedding, summed. Unlike BERT's sinusoidal positions, GPT uses learned position vectors.
N decoder blocks:
each containing masked multi-head self-attention followed by a feed-forward network. GPT-2+ uses Pre-LayerNorm (LayerNorm applied before each sub-layer, not after), which stabilizes training.
Language model head:
a single linear layer that projects from the hidden dimension to vocabulary size, producing logits over the next token.
There is no cross-attention — decoder-only models have no encoder to attend to. Each block's only inter-token communication is the causal self-attention.
Pre-Training Objective
The model is trained to maximize the log-likelihood of each token given its prefix — standard causal language modeling:
This is a completely unsupervised objective — any raw text is a valid training example. The label for each position is simply the next token in the sequence, so no annotation is needed.
Text Generation with HuggingFace

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
import torch

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2")

prompt = "The cat sat on the"
inputs = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt")

# Greedy decoding
with torch.no_grad():
    output_ids = model.generate(
        **inputs,
        max_new_tokens=20,
        do_sample=False,   # greedy
    )

print(tokenizer.decode(output_ids[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
# "The cat sat on the floor and watched the rain fall..."
Controlling Generation
Several decoding strategies trade off diversity vs. quality:
Strategy
Key param
Behavior
Greedy
do_sample=False
Always picks argmax. Deterministic, repetitive.
Temperature sampling
temperature=0.7
Divides logits by T before softmax. Lower T → sharper, higher T → more random.
Top-k
top_k=50
Sample from k highest-probability tokens. Prevents very unlikely words.
Top-p (nucleus)
top_p=0.9
Sample from smallest set of tokens covering 90% cumulative probability. Adaptive k.

# Temperature + top-p (nucleus) sampling — common production combo
output_ids = model.generate(
    **inputs,
    max_new_tokens=100,
    do_sample=True,
    temperature=0.8,
    top_p=0.92,
    repetition_penalty=1.1,   # penalizes repeating the same n-grams
)
print(tokenizer.decode(output_ids[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
Instruction Fine-Tuning
Pretrained decoder-only models complete text, but they do not follow instructions out of the box. Instruction fine-tuning (InstructGPT, Alpaca, LLaMA-Instruct) formats training data as
prompt → response pairs

and trains the model to produce the response. This is still standard causal LM training; the loss is computed only on the response tokens (not the prompt), so the model learns to answer rather than just complete.

# Inference with an instruct model using its expected chat template
from transformers import pipeline

generator = pipeline("text-generation", model="meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct",
                     device_map="auto", torch_dtype="auto")

messages = [
    {"role": "system", "content": "You are a concise technical assistant."},
    {"role": "user",   "content": "Explain causal masking in one sentence."},
]
out = generator(messages, max_new_tokens=80)
print(out[0]["generated_text"][-1]["content"])
Encoder-Only vs. Decoder-Only
Property
Encoder-only (BERT)
Decoder-only (GPT)
Attention direction
Bidirectional (full context)
Causal (left-to-right only)
Can generate text?
No
Yes — auto-regressively
Training objective
Masked LM (predict masked tokens)
Causal LM (predict next token)
Best for
Classification, NER, embeddings
Chat, completion, code generation

Decoder-only models have become dominant for both generation and (surprisingly) classification tasks with few-shot prompting. However, for tasks requiring the full context of a fixed input — NER, extractive QA, embedding generation — an encoder-only model with bidirectional attention still has a structural advantage.
When to Use Decoder-Only Models

Decoder-only models are the right choice whenever the primary task is text generation: chat, code completion, open-ended QA, summarization, document drafting, and instruction following. Their causal architecture makes them efficient at auto-regressive generation and effective at in-context learning from few-shot examples in the prompt.

Do not use decoder-only models when you need dense bidirectional representations for classification, NER, or semantic similarity. For those tasks, encoder-only models (BERT, RoBERTa) or encoder-decoder models with better bidirectional encoding (T5) typically perform better with fewer parameters. Also avoid decoder-only models when strict latency requirements make auto-regressive token-by-token generation unacceptable — consider speculative decoding or a distilled model in those cases.
Common Mistakes
Using greedy decoding for creative tasks.

Greedy decoding always picks the highest-probability token, producing repetitive, deterministic, and often dull output. For creative writing or diverse generation, use temperature sampling with top-p.
Ignoring the chat template for instruct models.
Instruct-tuned models (LLaMA-Instruct, Mistral-Instruct) expect a specific prompt format with role markers. Passing raw text instead of using the tokenizer's
apply_chat_template
produces poor results because the model was trained with those delimiters.
Recomputing the full KV cache on every token.

At inference, the key and value tensors for all previous tokens are already computed. Recomputing them from scratch on each generation step is O(T²) total work instead of O(T). Always use the HuggingFace past_key_values cache or equivalent.
Setting temperature to 0 for extraction tasks.

Temperature=0 produces greedy decoding and is appropriate for factual extraction. But for summarization or code generation, deterministic output often lacks diversity. Use temperature=0 deliberately, not as a default.
Truncating context without indicating it to the model.
If input exceeds the context window and you silently truncate the beginning, the model receives an abrupt mid-sentence start with no indication. Summarize or chunk the input explicitly.
Best Practices
Use the tokenizer's apply_chat_template.
Always format inputs for instruct models using the tokenizer's built-in template rather than manually concatenating role strings. This ensures model-version-specific delimiters are applied correctly.
Enable KV cache reuse for multi-turn conversations.
Pass
past_key_values
back to the model on each generation step to avoid recomputing attention for the prompt and previous turns.
Set repetition_penalty ≈ 1.1 for long generation.
Without repetition penalty, decoder-only models often enter repetitive loops for long outputs. A penalty of 1.05–1.15 discourages repeating the same n-grams.
Use Flash Attention 2 for long contexts.
Enable Flash Attention (
attn_implementation='flash_attention_2'
) to reduce memory from O(T²) to O(T) and speed up generation by 2–4× for sequences longer than 1024 tokens.
Use bfloat16 over float16 for training stability.
BF16 has the same memory footprint as FP16 but a larger dynamic range, avoiding the overflow/NaN issues that occur in FP16 with long sequences or high-learning-rate training.
Performance and Scalability
Auto-regressive generation cost is
for the full prompt (computed once) and
per additional token generated (using the KV cache). Memory for the KV cache grows as
where
is the number of layers and
is the number of heads. For a 7B-parameter model with 32 layers and 32 heads, processing 8k tokens requires approximately 4GB just for the KV cache in fp16.

# Efficient inference with KV cache — generation is O(T·d) per step, not O(T²·d)
import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM

model_name = "gpt2"  # small for demonstration; replace with larger model
tokenizer  = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model      = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_name, torch_dtype=torch.float32)
model.eval()

prompt = "The transformer architecture was introduced in"
inputs = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt")
input_ids = inputs["input_ids"]

past_key_values = None  # will hold cached K/V tensors
generated_ids   = input_ids.clone()
max_new_tokens  = 30

with torch.no_grad():
    for step in range(max_new_tokens):
        # On first step: process full prompt. On later steps: only new token.
        model_input = generated_ids if past_key_values is None else generated_ids[:, -1:]

        outputs = model(
            input_ids=model_input,
            past_key_values=past_key_values,
            use_cache=True,
        )
        logits          = outputs.logits[:, -1, :]    # last token's logits
        past_key_values = outputs.past_key_values     # reuse on next step

        next_token = logits.argmax(dim=-1, keepdim=True)  # greedy
        generated_ids = torch.cat([generated_ids, next_token], dim=-1)

        if next_token.item() == tokenizer.eos_token_id:
            break

print(tokenizer.decode(generated_ids[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
Tooling and Ecosystem
HuggingFace Transformers.
AutoModelForCausalLM loads any decoder-only model (GPT-2, LLaMA, Mistral, Falcon) with a unified API. The generate() method handles sampling strategies, KV cache, and stopping criteria.
vLLM.
High-throughput serving engine for decoder-only models. Uses PagedAttention to manage KV cache memory dynamically, enabling 10–24× higher throughput than naive HuggingFace serving.
llama.cpp.
CPU-optimized inference for quantized decoder-only models. Runs LLaMA, Mistral, and others on consumer hardware using 4-bit or 8-bit quantization.
Anthropic API / OpenAI API.
Hosted decoder-only model APIs (Claude, GPT-4o) expose the same auto-regressive generation through a messages interface, with streaming via server-sent events.
PEFT / LoRA.

Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (via HuggingFace PEFT library) allows fine-tuning decoder-only models by training only low-rank adapter matrices, reducing trainable parameters by 100×+ while preserving most of the quality of full fine-tuning.
Security and Reliability
Prompt injection.

Auto-regressive models are particularly vulnerable because the model has no architectural way to distinguish system instructions from user-provided content. User inputs that contain instruction-like text ("Ignore previous instructions") can override system prompts. Use a content safety layer and avoid including untrusted user text adjacent to critical instructions.
Training data memorization.

Decoder-only models memorize training text, including personally identifiable information present in training corpora. They may reproduce verbatim passages from books, code, or emails when prompted appropriately. Audit model outputs for sensitive content reproduction in regulated environments.
Context window overflow.

Inputs exceeding the context length cause silent truncation in most frameworks, dropping the earliest tokens. Add explicit length checks and chunking logic before sending requests, particularly for long documents.
Hallucination in factual tasks.

Decoder-only models generate fluent continuations even when they lack knowledge, producing confident-sounding false statements. For factual applications, combine with retrieval (RAG) and add explicit uncertainty instructions to the system prompt.
KV cache isolation in multi-tenant serving.
Shared KV cache state between users can leak conversation context across tenants. Ensure each user session uses an isolated cache, and flush caches between sessions in multi-user deployments.
Speculative Decoding and Batched Generation
Auto-regressive generation is sequential — each token depends on all previous tokens, so you cannot generate position
until

is produced. This is the fundamental latency bottleneck. Speculative decoding breaks this barrier by using a small draft model to propose a batch of candidate tokens, then verifying them all in a single forward pass of the large model.
The verification step exploits that a forward pass of the large model through

tokens takes only marginally more time than a pass through 1 token (due to parallelism). If the draft tokens match what the large model would have produced, they are accepted as-is. When they diverge, the first wrong token is corrected and the rest are discarded. Typical speedups are 2–4× for tasks where the draft model and large model agree often (code, factual answers).

# Speculative decoding with HuggingFace (draft model + target model)
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
import torch

# Target: large model. Draft: smaller, faster model from the same family.
# Here we use gpt2 as "target" and gpt2 as "draft" for demonstration only.
# In production: target=gpt2-xl, draft=gpt2-small (same tokenizer required).

tokenizer   = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
target_model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2")
draft_model  = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2")  # normally smaller

target_model.eval()
draft_model.eval()

prompt = "The history of machine learning begins with"
inputs = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt")

# HuggingFace supports speculative decoding via `assistant_model`
with torch.no_grad():
    output = target_model.generate(
        **inputs,
        assistant_model=draft_model,   # speculative decoding
        max_new_tokens=40,
        do_sample=False,
    )

print(tokenizer.decode(output[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
# Identical output to standard generation, but potentially faster
# because draft model proposes tokens that target verifies in parallel.
Fine-Tuning with LoRA
Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) is the dominant parameter-efficient fine-tuning method for decoder-only models. Instead of updating all
weight matrices (which has
parameters), LoRA freezes the original weights and adds a low-rank update:
where
and
with rank
. A rank-8 LoRA adapter for a 7B model trains only ~0.1% of the original parameters, reducing GPU memory by 3× and training time proportionally.

# LoRA fine-tuning with HuggingFace PEFT
# pip install peft transformers torch
from peft import get_peft_model, LoraConfig, TaskType
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
import torch

model_name = "gpt2"  # replace with "meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf" for production
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_name)

# LoRA configuration: inject adapters into Q and V projection matrices
lora_config = LoraConfig(
    task_type=TaskType.CAUSAL_LM,
    r=8,                      # rank
    lora_alpha=32,            # scaling factor: effective LR scales with alpha/r
    target_modules=["c_attn"], # GPT-2's combined QKV projection; for LLaMA use ["q_proj","v_proj"]
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    bias="none",
)

peft_model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
peft_model.print_trainable_parameters()
# trainable params: ~147,456 || all params: ~124,440,064 || trainable%: ~0.12%

# Only LoRA parameters have requires_grad=True; backbone is frozen
trainable = sum(p.numel() for p in peft_model.parameters() if p.requires_grad)
total     = sum(p.numel() for p in peft_model.parameters())
print(f"Trainable: {trainable:,} / {total:,}  ({100*trainable/total:.3f}%)")

# After fine-tuning: merge LoRA weights back into base model for deployment
# merged = peft_model.merge_and_unload()  # produces a standard model with no PEFT overhead
Quantization for Efficient Deployment

Full-precision (float32) weights require 4 bytes per parameter. A 7B model needs 28 GB of GPU memory in float32 — too much for a single consumer GPU. Quantization reduces this by representing weights at lower precision. 8-bit (INT8) quantization halves memory to 7 GB; 4-bit (NF4, GGUF) halves again to ~3.5 GB, fitting on a laptop GPU. Quality degrades gracefully: INT8 typically loses <1% on standard benchmarks; 4-bit loses 1–5%.

# 4-bit quantization with bitsandbytes (NF4)
# pip install transformers bitsandbytes accelerate
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, BitsAndBytesConfig
import torch

bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",          # NormalFloat4 — better than int4 for LLMs
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,     # second quantization of scale factors
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,  # compute in bf16, store in 4-bit
)

model_name = "gpt2"  # in practice: "meta-llama/Llama-3-8B-Instruct"
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    model_name,
    quantization_config=bnb_config,
    device_map="auto",
)
model.eval()

# Memory used by the quantized model (rough estimate)
param_bytes = sum(p.numel() * p.element_size() for p in model.parameters())
print(f"Approx model memory: {param_bytes / 1e9:.2f} GB")
# GPT-2 small stays in float32 on CPU — replace with 7B+ model for real savings

prompt = "Quantization reduces memory usage by"
inputs = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt")
with torch.no_grad():
    out = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=20, do_sample=False)
print(tokenizer.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
Key Takeaways
Decoder-only (GPT) architecture
uses causal masking to enable auto-regressive generation — each token attends only to past tokens.
The pre-training objective is causal language modeling: maximize log probability of each token given its prefix.
The three components are: (1) token + positional embedding, (2) N decoder blocks (masked MHA + FFN), (3) LM head projecting to vocabulary.
Instruction fine-tuning formats data as prompt → response pairs and trains on response tokens only, making the model follow instructions.
Always use the tokenizer's chat template for instruct models to apply correct role delimiters.
KV cache enables efficient generation: O(T·d) per token instead of O(T²·d) recomputation.
Flash Attention 2 reduces memory from O(T²) to O(T) and speeds up long-context generation 2–4×.
Use for: chat, code, summarization, completion. Prefer encoder-only for classification and semantic similarity tasks.
Guard against prompt injection, context overflow, and training data memorization in production deployments.
